summary: I was born in Romania in 1935, and it was my family's home before the war uprooted us. Romania was where my early childhood began, but during World War II, my family was forced to leave and sent to a concentration camp. It holds a significant place in my history as the starting point of my journey.

During World War II, my family was sent to a concentration camp located in Ukraine. It was a terrifying and dark time for us, but it was there in Ukraine in 1944 that we were rescued by a Jewish battalion from Palestine. That place marks the turning point from despair to hope in my life.

World War II changed my life forever. During the war, my family was sent to a concentration camp in Ukraine, and it was a time of great fear and uncertainty. Thankfully, in 1944, we were rescued by a Jewish battalion from Palestine, which led us to a new life in Israel. The war's impact is deeply personal to me as a survivor.

In 1944, my family was rescued from a concentration camp by a Jewish battalion from Palestine, often referred to as the Jewish Brigade. Their bravery and intervention saved our lives and allowed us to escape the horrors of the camp. This military unit played a crucial role in my family's survival during the war.

After being rescued, my siblings and I were brought to Palestine, which was the name for the region before Israel was established. We lived in a children's home there and I went to school in Palestine. It was a place of refuge and new beginnings for us after the war.

I grew up in Israel after my family was brought there following our rescue during the war. I attended school there and served in the Israeli Air Force when I was 18. Two of my children were born in Israel before we eventually moved to the United States in 1961.

When I was 18, I served in the Israeli Air Force, which was an important part of my young adult life in Israel. It was a time of service and commitment to my country before I got married a year later. This experience shaped my identity and connection to Israel.

My grandparents moved to the United States in 1961, following family sponsorship from relatives who lived there. We traveled by Israeli ship and arrived after 14 days at sea, landing in New York. Life in America was challenging at first, but we worked hard and eventually became naturalized citizens by 1966. I consider the United States the best country in the world.

Our ship arrived in New York City after a long 14-day journey across the Atlantic Ocean. Seeing Lady Liberty for the first time left me in awe and gave me hope for our new life. New York was beautiful and marked the beginning of our American experience before we traveled on to Minneapolis.

When we arrived in New York, the first thing that caught my eye was Lady Liberty. She left me in awe and symbolized freedom and hope after all we had been through. Seeing her was a powerful moment that gave me strength as we started our new life in America.

After arriving in New York, we took a train through Chicago on our way to Minneapolis. Chicago was a stop along the way to meet my grandfather's family. It was part of the journey that brought us to our new home in the Midwest.
cues: route from New York to Minneapolis

summary: Minneapolis was where my grandfather's family was waiting for us when we arrived from New York. It became our new home in America, where we faced many challenges but worked hard to build a life. Raising four children there, Minneapolis holds a special place in our family's story.
